entries.description: Secret funding of lawsuits to bankrupt Gawker Media (2012–2016): Thiel provided millions in funding for Hulk Hogan's privacy lawsuit against Gawker (over a sex tape), motivated partly by Gawker outing him as gay in 2007. The $140M verdict led to Gawker's bankruptcy and shutdown, raising concerns about wealthy individuals using litigation to suppress media outlets.
entries.causation_evidence: Forbes, NYT reporting; court records in Bollea v. Gawker

entries.description: Co-founding and long-term involvement with Palantir Technologies (2003–2026): Palantir's data analytics and AI surveillance tools, sold to governments (including CIA early backing, ICE, DoD, IRS), have enabled mass surveillance, predictive policing, immigrant tracking/deportations, military targeting, and creation of integrated federal databases (including IRS tax data concerns in 2025–2026). Expanded contracts under Trump administration raised privacy and authoritarian overreach alarms.
entries.causation_evidence: Guardian, NYT, Reuters reporting; USASpending.gov; congressional scrutiny; former employee concerns

entries.description: Extensive political funding and influence network (2016–2026): Thiel donated millions (e.g., ~$15M to JD Vance's Senate campaign), mentored Vance, introduced him to Trump, and helped secure Vance's VP role. Network of protégés gained influence in Trump administration (including DOGE ties). Thiel's public views (e.g., 'freedom and democracy are not compatible') and support for aligned candidates raised concerns about concentrated elite power and anti-democratic tendencies.
entries.causation_evidence: Campaign finance records; NYT, Washington Post reporting on Thiel-Vance-Trump ties

entries.description: Public lectures framing regulation and opponents as 'Antichrist' (2025–2026): Thiel delivered talks (including in San Francisco, Paris, and Rome) portraying critics of unchecked tech progress, climate activists (e.g., references to Greta Thunberg), and regulators as part of apocalyptic 'Antichrist' forces. Vatican and Catholic scholars criticized the rhetoric as inflammatory or heretical, potentially polarizing discourse on AI safety and ethics.
entries.causation_evidence: NYT, AP, Fortune reporting on Rome lectures and prior talks; Vatican responses

entries.description: Palantir's role in Trump administration data integration and immigration enforcement (2025–2026): Palantir secured expanded federal contracts (hundreds of millions, including with ICE for migrant tracking, IRS for data analysis, and cross-agency databases). Critics alleged facilitation of surveillance state elements, privacy risks to taxpayers, and support for aggressive deportation policies amid protests and UN concerns over related activities.
entries.causation_evidence: NYT, Guardian, Reuters; US government contract records; congressional letters

entries.description: Palantir named in UN report on profiting from conflict (Gaza context, 2025): Palantir was listed among companies allegedly profiting from activities linked to the Gaza conflict through data/surveillance tools. Thiel's foundational role and ongoing stake drew criticism for indirect involvement in militarized tech amid human rights concerns.
entries.causation_evidence: UN report mentions; Red Flag, Guardian coverage

entries.description: Rome Antichrist lecture series sparking Vatican backlash (March 2026): Thiel hosted invitation-only lectures near the Vatican on the Antichrist, AI, and end-times themes, framing certain forces as existential threats. Catholic institutions distanced themselves; critics called it scandalous or heretical, intensifying debates on Thiel's influence blending tech, politics, and eschatology.
entries.causation_evidence: AP, NYT, America Magazine reporting on the event and responses

entries.description: Palantir's aggressive government contracting and ideological manifesto (2025–2026): Palantir published positions supporting AI-powered military tools and criticizing 'hollow pluralism.' Combined with Thiel's influence, this fueled protests, Democratic contract concerns, and accusations of advancing technocratic authoritarianism.
entries.causation_evidence: Forbes, Techdirt, academic responses (e.g., Cas Mudde)

entries.description: Founding and expansion of Thiel Fellowship (2011–2026): Program awards substantial grants ($100k–$250k over two years, doubled for 2026 class) to young people (under ~23) to skip/drop out of college and pursue startups, research, or bold projects. 2026 class highlighted global diversity in FinTech, robotics, AI; thousands of applicants, hundreds of fellows with notable alumni successes.
entries.causation_evidence: Thiel Foundation announcements; BusinessWire, official site

entries.description: Co-founding PayPal and early transformative investments (1998–2026): Co-founded and led PayPal (sold to eBay); first major outside investor in Facebook; backed SpaceX, LinkedIn, Yelp, Airbnb, Stripe via Founders Fund. Created 'PayPal Mafia' network that spawned multiple high-impact companies advancing digital payments, social media, and space tech.
entries.causation_evidence: Public records; Wikipedia; Founders Fund history

entries.description: Thiel Foundation and targeted philanthropy (2006–2026): Supports long-term thinking, science, technology via grants for anti-aging/longevity research (e.g., SENS, Methuselah), AI safety (e.g., MIRI), seasteading experiments, Imitatio (René Girard studies), and contrarian projects. Annual disbursements promote high-risk innovation outside mainstream channels.
entries.causation_evidence: Thiel Foundation site; Inside Philanthropy; public grant reports

entries.description: Co-founding Founders Fund venture capital firm (2005–2026): Firm has backed numerous high-growth companies (SpaceX, Airbnb, Stripe, and later AI/defense/biotech bets). Thiel's involvement emphasized 'zero to one' innovation, monopoly-building as progress driver, and funding bold, contrarian technologies.
entries.causation_evidence: Founders Fund records; Thiel's 'Zero to One'; investment announcements

entries.description: 2026 Thiel Fellowship class expansion: Doubled grant size to $250k per fellow for a focused, globally diverse cohort emphasizing disruptive fields (FinTech, robotics, AI). Statement highlighted rejecting traditional 'Boomer' paths in favor of iconoclastic building.
